retrieved_docs.title: Left Ventricular Assist Devices -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Right, ventricular dysfunction: Either primarily or right heart failure, not secondary to left heart failure. Improper function of the right ventricle leads to insufficient filling of the left heart, which may lead to inadequate inflow in the device and, ultimately, device malfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: Right Heart Failure -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: RVF is most often due to LVF, and patients with biventricular failure have a 2-year survival of 23%, compared to 71% in patients with LVF alone. [4] [5] In the CHARITEM registry, RVF accounted for 2.2% of heart failure admissions and was secondary to LVF in more than 1/5 of cases. The Egyptian Heart Failure-LT registry reported that 4.5% of patients with acutely decompensated heart failure had RVF, compared to 3% in other regions under the European Society of Cardiology. This difference may be attributed to the higher prevalence of rheumatic heart disease in the region.

retrieved_docs.title: Right Heart Failure -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Like the left ventricle, contraction of the right ventricle depends on preload under normal physiologic filling pressures. Excessive filling of the right ventricle can cause the septum to shift toward the left ventricle, leading to ventricular interdependence and impaired left ventricular function.

retrieved_docs.title: Double-Chambered Right Ventricle -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Complications after surgery are not commonly observed. However, after surgery, patients may be at risk for death from low cardiac output states or heart failure despite successful resection of the anomalous muscle bundle. They may also experience arrhythmias and right ventricular dysfunction. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiac Dominance -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Left-heart dominance was described to be a high-risk feature for percutaneous coronary intervention as well. Left-heart–dominant patients undergoing percutaneous coronary intervention have higher in-hospital mortality and are more likely to present with symptoms of heart failure, cardiogenic shock, or cardiac arrest. [8] Left-heart dominance in patients undergoing aortic valve replacement for severe aortic stenosis was also shown to be an independent risk factor. There appears to be an increased prevalence of left-heart dominance in patients undergoing aortic valve replacement, and the left main coronary artery is significantly shorter than those seen in right-heart–dominant patients; this may be evidence of a developmental relationship seen in left-heart–dominant patients between the left main coronary artery and aortic valve. In one study, patients with left dominant coronary circulation were determined to suffer a significantly higher rate of perioperative myocardial infarction compared to right heart dominant or codominant patients. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Right Heart Failure -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: However, the hypertrophy and maintenance of stroke volume are insufficient to normalize wall stress, leading to right ventricular dilation. This dilation is accompanied by rising filling pressures, decreased contractility, loss of synchronicity as the right ventricle becomes more spherical, and dilation of the tricuspid valve annulus. As a result, the valve leaflets fail to coapt properly, causing functional tricuspid regurgitation, which worsens right ventricular volume overload, right ventricular enlargement (RVE), wall stress, and problems with contractility and cardiac output.

retrieved_docs.title: Right Heart Failure -- Evaluation -- Echocardiography
retrieved_docs.content: Right ventricular free wall strain: This tool is also useful in assessing right ventricular function. Strain is a composite measurement of right ventricular loading and dysfunction. Abnormal strain patterns have been associated with disease progression, higher diuretic doses, and mortality in PAH. Right ventricular strain has also been shown to predict RVF after implantation of a left ventricular assist device (LVAD) and clinical outcomes in patients referred for heart transplantation.

retrieved_docs.title: Right Heart Failure -- Evaluation -- Nuclear angiography
retrieved_docs.content: First-pass radionuclide ventriculography was, for a long time, the gold standard for measuring right ventricular ejection fraction (RVEF). A bolus of the technetium-99m tracer is injected, and a sequence of cardiac cycles is acquired as the bolus passes through the heart. A normal RVEF is 52% plus or minus 6%, with 40% considered the lower limit of normal. Nuclear angiography is limited by its inability to measure right ventricular volumes and its sensitivity to cardiac arrhythmia.

retrieved_docs.title: Right Heart Failure -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Right heart failure is a condition that arises when the right ventricle cannot efficiently pump deoxygenated blood to the lungs for oxygenation before it returns to the left side of the heart to supply the rest of the body. Right ventricular failure (RVF) often accompanies left ventricular failure (LVF), though these disease entities are distinct. The right ventricle differs structurally from the left ventricle and is vulnerable to various disease processes. While RVF has multiple causes, the most common is LVF, which increases pulmonary vascular pressure, leading to pressure or volume overload in the right ventricle. [1] [2] This activity examines the right ventricle's normal structure and function. The pathophysiology and the medical and surgical management of RVF will also be reviewed.

retrieved_docs.title: Left Ventricular Failure -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The heart is comprised of the pericardium, myocardium, and endocardium. Pathology in any of those structures can lead to heart failure. Left ventricular failure occurs when there is dysfunction of the left ventricle causing insufficient delivery of blood to vital body organs. Left ventricular failure can further subdivide into heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF with EF over 50%), heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF with EF less than 40%), or heart failure with mid-range ejection fraction (EF between 41 and 49 percent).

retrieved_docs.title: Sudden Cardiac Death -- Etiology -- Heart Failure
retrieved_docs.content: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction

retrieved_docs.title: Right Heart Failure -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Due to lower right-sided pressures and wall stress, the oxygen requirement of the right ventricle is lower than that of the left ventricle. Coronary blood flow and oxygen extraction are also lower in the right ventricle. Consequently, the right ventricle is less vulnerable to ischemic damage. Increases in oxygen demand in the right ventricle are compensated by increased coronary flow or higher oxygen extraction, as seen in PAH cases or during exercise. Right ventricular function is influenced by atrial contraction, heart rate, and synchronicity. Each factor significantly impacts right ventricular function and has important clinical implications.

retrieved_docs.title: Heart Failure and Ejection Fraction -- History and Physical -- Signs of Heart Failure:
retrieved_docs.content: Pulses alternans Elevated jugular venous pressure Displaced LV apex beat Cardiac cachexia Sinus tachycardia Right ventricular heave Bilateral rales/ crepitations or a cardiac wheeze on lung exam Presence of S3 Pitting edema in dependent areas Tender hepatomegaly Ascites

retrieved_docs.title: Left Ventricular Failure -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple variables have associations with worse outcome in heart failure: male gender, advancing age, low ejection fraction, high NYHA functional class, low hematocrit, and sodium levels, high brain natriuretic peptide, low peak exercise oxygen uptake, wide QRS, renal failure, low blood pressure, elevated heart rate, and volume overload refractory to medical treatment. [19] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Right Heart Failure -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Clinically, patients present with the signs and symptoms of hypoxemia and systemic venous congestion. These manifestations include breathlessness, chest discomfort, palpitations, and swelling. Meanwhile, common physical examination findings include the following: Jugular venous distension Hepatojugular reflux Peripheral edema Hepatosplenomegaly, often with hepatic pulsation Ascites Anasarca S3 gallop Tricuspid regurgitation murmur Right ventricular heave Signs of concomitant LVF Paradoxical pulse

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 170
retrieved_docs.content: around the heart and pericardium. “Other or heart procedures” and the broader category HEART: GENERAL AND MISCELLANEOUS OPERATIVE PROCEDURES include cardiac pacing (demand pacing, implantation of rate‑responsive single‑chamber devices, replacement with dual‑chamber devices, and lead removal), cardioversion (intracardiac‑catheter and elective external electrical), carotid‑sinus stimulation with simultaneous electrocardiographic monitoring, temporary cardio‑assist with an artificial heart, whole‑body plethysmography, and open‑heart surgery. Diagnostic cardiac catheterization, coronary arteriography and related diagnostic cardiovascular procedures form the parent categories for “Diagnostic procedures on heart and pericardium,” which encompass heart biopsy and its sub‑procedures (open left‑ventricular biopsy, open right‑ventricular biopsy, transluminal left‑ventricular biopsy). Heart biopsy serves as a central hub linking diagnostic interventions to HEART: EXCISIONS and to the inverse‑ISA

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2291
retrieved_docs.content: HEART: GENERAL AND MISCELLANEOUS OPERATIVE PROCEDURES is a broad surgical category that encompasses several specific cardiac interventions. Among its subprocedures are Temporary cardioassist with artificial heart, which involves the use of a mechanical device to provide short‑term circulatory support when the native heart cannot maintain adequate output, and Operation on heart and pericardium, which includes surgical techniques directed at repairing or modifying the myocardium, valves, coronary vessels, or the surrounding pericardial sac. Together, these child procedures illustrate the range of operative options available for managing acute cardiac failure, structural heart disease, and pericardial pathology within the overarching framework of general and miscellaneous heart surgery.

retrieved_docs.content: Shock, a clinical syndrome of inadequate tissue perfusion, is classified as صَدْمَة قَلْبِيَّة المَنْشَأ, which denotes cardiogenic shock—a form of shock that originates from primary cardiac dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 186
retrieved_docs.content: two major sub‑classifications are recognized. One subtype lacks explicit complications and is classified as diabetes mellitus without complication. The other subtype is characterized by diabetic peripheral angiopathy with gangrene and is considered a subset of diabetes mellitus with complications, underscoring the broader impact of poorly controlled diabetes on overall health. Organ‑level functional failures commonly co‑occur with secondary diabetes mellitus, including rheumatic mitral valve failure and hepatic‑renal failure, both of which contribute to the patient’s general clinical state. The uncontrolled disease condition reflected in the general clinical state is specifically linked to diabetes mellitus with complications, highlighting the systemic burden of inadequately managed diabetes. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1114
retrieved_docs.content: السفلي are noted in the same terminology sets, and acute and unspecified renal failure is reported to be related, possibly synonymous, with lower‑agent entities (كلاء الوكيل السفلي), suggesting a broader systemic context for patients with cardiac conduction abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1041
retrieved_docs.content: Failure (biologic function) is closely linked to several serious clinical conditions. It is associated with rheumatic mitral valve failure, indicating that dysfunction of biological systems can contribute to or coexist with valvular heart disease. In addition, Failure (biologic function) is associated with الفَشَلٌ الكَبِدِيُّ الكُلْوِيّ (hepatic‑renal failure), highlighting a connection between systemic organ failure and combined liver‑kidney impairment. Moreover, Failure (biologic function) interprets the General clinical state, suggesting that the presence of functional failure influences the overall clinical assessment of a patient. The General clinical state, in turn, is used to interpret Disease condition determination, uncontrolled, reflecting how clinicians evaluate the severity and stability of a disease when it is not well‑controlled. Disease condition determination, uncontrolled is associated with diabetes mellitus with complications, underscoring that uncontrolled disease status frequently co‑occurs with complicated diabetes. Together, these relationships illustrate how biological failure, specific organ pathologies, and uncontrolled disease conditions are interrelated and shape the general clinical picture, which is essential for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 233
retrieved_docs.content: is linked to loss of biological function (Failure) and may occur concurrently with the rapid cardiac inflammation of acute rheumatic conditions, underscoring the potential for extra‑cardiac organ dysfunction. Overall, the knowledge base depicts rheumatic fever and its cardiac sequelae as a tightly connected group of entities—ranging from valve‑specific failures (particularly mitral and tricuspid) to diffuse myocardial and pericardial inflammation—typically presenting abruptly and briefly, with possible simultaneous hepatic and renal compromise.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2896
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac function is a broad physiological concept that includes measures such as معدل ضربات القلب (heart rate). In clinical practice, the evaluation of cardiac function is interpreted specifically in the context of congestive heart failure in patients who are non‑hypertensive, providing important information for diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2613
retrieved_docs.content: Failure (biologic function) is linked to both Rheumatic mitral valve failure and الفَشَلٌ الكَبِدِيُّ الكُلْوِيّ. Rheumatic mitral valve failure refers to damage of the mitral valve caused by rheumatic heart disease, leading to impaired cardiac function. الفَشَلٌ الكَبِدِيُّ الكُلْوِيّ denotes a combined hepatic‑renal failure, a severe condition in which both liver and kidney functions are compromised. The association of general biologic function failure with these distinct organ‑specific failures underscores the interconnected nature of systemic physiological decline, where dysfunction in one system may reflect or contribute to serious cardiac valvular disease as well as simultaneous liver and kidney impairment. Recognizing these relationships can aid clinicians in comprehensive assessment and integrated management of patients presenting with multi‑organ failure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 1242
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to and may be considered synonymous with the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. Within this group of nonspecific clinical manifestations, the specific finding of الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse) is recognized as a child element, illustrating that severe cardiovascular failure can be one of the many possible presentations encompassed by ill‑defined symptom complexes. This relationship underscores the importance of viewing individual symptoms as part of a larger, sometimes vague, clinical picture when assessing patients with ambiguous or poorly characterized disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2663
retrieved_docs.content: Open left ventricular biopsy and open right ventricular biopsy are both classified as خزعة القلب (heart biopsy) and are performed within the framework of جراحة القلب المفتوح (open‑heart surgery). These procedures involve surgically accessing the left or right ventricular chamber, respectively, to obtain myocardial tissue samples for diagnostic evaluation. By being both a type of heart biopsy and an integral component of open‑heart surgery, they illustrate how tissue sampling is incorporated into invasive cardiac operative techniques to inform patient management and therapeutic decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 148
retrieved_docs.content: network. Systemic comorbidities are highlighted by the overlap of acute and unspecified renal failure with lower‑limb agent exposure (كلاء الكليون السفلي). Together, these interrelated entities provide a comprehensive framework for the diagnosis, classification, and management of cardiac electrophysiology disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2478
retrieved_docs.content: Failure (biologic function) is a central concept that links specific organ dysfunctions and overall patient assessment. It is associated with rheumatic mitral valve failure, indicating that impairment of the biological function can manifest as damage to the mitral valve caused by rheumatic disease. It is also associated with الفَشَلٌ الكَبِدِيُّ الكُلْوِيّ, reflecting a combined hepatic‑renal failure in which both liver and kidney functions are compromised. In clinical practice, the presence and extent of such biologic failure are used to interpret the general clinical state, providing clinicians with insight into the patient’s overall health status and guiding comprehensive management strategies.
